1: A theme: one dead fly in the perfumer's ointment corrupts the whole. The proverbial logic applies to wisdom itself — a single folly can undo an established reputation. The NT develops this as a warning: a little leaven leavens the whole lump (1 Corinthians 5:6; Galatians 5:9). Christ alone is the one whose wisdom is untainted; the one who knew no sin (2 Corinthians 5:21) is the only teacher whose instruction is not contaminated by the folly it diagnoses.
2: A theme: the wise heart inclines to the right, the fool's to the left. This spatial metaphor for moral orientation runs through wisdom literature. The NT locates Christ at the right hand of God (Hebrews 1:3; Colossians 3:1), the definitive orientation of all true wisdom. The cross, which appeared as folly and weakness — a thing of the left — was in fact the right hand of God's power (1 Corinthians 1:24-25).
3: A theme: the fool's folly is self-revealing even in ordinary travel — his judgment fails and he broadcasts his folly to everyone. The NT applies the same exposure to those who reject the wisdom of God: what is hidden is made manifest (Luke 8:17). The cross, conversely, exposes the folly of the wise: 'God chose the foolish things of the world to shame the wise' (1 Corinthians 1:27).
4: A revelation of God: composure can defuse even serious offenses against a ruler. The verse reveals a dimension of God's character — his patience and the possibility of de-escalation even when anger is justified. Christ embodies this composure before Pilate: 'He gave him no answer' (John 19:9), and before his accusers he was led as a sheep to slaughter, not opening his mouth (Acts 8:32, citing Isaiah 53:7). The composure that secures pardon is his.
5: A theme: there is a persistent blunder observable in human societies — those in power make disordered appointments, elevating fools and sidelining the capable. The Preacher names it as an evil 'under the sun.' The NT traces this disorder to the fall and its corrupted power structures (Romans 8:20-21; 1 Corinthians 2:6-8: the rulers of this age who crucified the Lord of glory). All human governance operates within this fallen disorder until Christ's kingdom comes.
6: A revelation of God: fools in high positions, the capable in lowly ones. This inversion of proper order is one of the book's recurring social observations. The NT's great reversal — the Magnificat's 'he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate' (Luke 1:52) — is the eschatological answer: the order disrupted by the fall will be restored, and the capable who were assigned lowly places will be exalted in the kingdom of the one who himself took the lowest place (Philippians 2:7-11).
7: A theme: the servant class riding and the noble class walking — social order inverted. This observation connects to the kingdom's inversion of rank: Christ came 'not to be served but to serve' (Mark 10:45), taking the servant's position himself. The one to whom all service is owed washed his disciples' feet (John 13:1-17); the social inversion the Preacher laments will be finally corrected when every knee bows (Philippians 2:10-11).
8: A theme: whoever digs a pit may fall in it; whoever breaks through a wall may be bitten. The irony of evil returning upon its author runs through Wisdom literature (Proverbs 26:27). The cross enacts this irony definitively: those who plotted Christ's death and demanded the curse 'his blood be on us and our children' (Matthew 27:25) found the very condemnation they sought to impose turned back; the one who fell into the pit they dug rose out of it (Acts 2:24).
9: A theme: labor under fallen conditions carries inherent danger — quarrying and wood-splitting wound the worker. Christ's own labor was not exempt from this cost: he 'learned obedience through what he suffered' (Hebrews 5:8), and the work of redemption was accomplished through the wounds of the cross (1 Peter 2:24). The danger that attends all work 'under the sun' was borne by the one who entered the world to undo the curse that made it so.
10: A theme: wisdom gives the axe its edge and makes effort succeed where brute force fails. The NT redefines this: the wisdom of God is not a technique but a person (1 Corinthians 1:24). Christ does not overcome through worldly force — 'not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit' (Zechariah 4:6, echoed in the NT's theology of weakness). The cross, the apparent opposite of 'wisdom making success,' is in fact the power by which God accomplished what the law's brute force could not (Romans 8:3-4).
11: A theme: if a serpent bites before it is charmed, the charmer has wasted his effort; words uncontrolled do the same damage. The tongue as a serpent is a wisdom motif developed most extensively in James 3:3-12: the tongue 'is a restless evil, full of deadly poison.' Christ's words are the contrast — controlled, healing, and life-giving (John 6:63: the words I have spoken to you are spirit and life). He tames the serpent whose bite Ecclesiastes fears.
12: A revelation of God: the wise person's words earn goodwill while the fool's words devour him. The divine standard of speech — words that heal and build — is embodied in Christ: 'No one ever spoke the way this man speaks' (John 7:46); 'all spoke well of him and were amazed at the gracious words that came from his lips' (Luke 4:22). His words earn goodwill even as they expose what the heart has hid (Luke 2:35).
13: A theme: the fool's speech begins with foolishness and ends in wicked madness — a trajectory of deterioration. The NT traces this same downward arc in the rejection of wisdom: Romans 1:21-32 describes how, beginning with the failure to honor God, the mind becomes futile and descends into disorder. Christ is Wisdom calling out from every corner (Proverbs 1:20-21; Matthew 11:19: wisdom is proved right by her deeds), offering the alternative to this trajectory before it reaches its end.
14: A revelation of God: the fool multiplies words about what no human can know — the future. This verse places human knowledge of the future firmly in God's sole possession. Christ himself acknowledged this boundary with respect to his own earthly knowledge: 'But about that day or hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father' (Matthew 24:36). The Preacher and Jesus agree: presuming to know God's timetable is folly; only the Father knows.
15: A theme: the fool's exhausting labor cannot even accomplish the basic task of finding the city. The fruitlessness of effort disconnected from wisdom is the Preacher's recurring observation. Christ's invitation is the direct counterpoint: 'Come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest' (Matthew 11:28). The labor that exhausts without arriving is replaced by the yoke that is easy and the burden that is light (Matthew 11:29-30), because it is wisdom's own yoke.
16: A revelation of God: woe to the land whose king is a boy — immature, self-indulgent — and whose rulers carouse in the morning. The Preacher sets out the divine standard for kingship: maturity, self-discipline, and governance for the people's good. Christ is measured against this standard and found to fulfill it perfectly: the King who fasted forty days (Matthew 4:2), who refused to use his power for self-indulgence (Matthew 4:3-4), and who served rather than caroused (John 13:1-17).
17: A shadow: blessed is the land whose king is of noble character and whose princes eat for strength, not excess. This portrait of the righteous king and ordered court anticipates the kingdom of God. Under Christ's reign — the king of noble character whose every action serves the kingdom's flourishing — the blessing the Preacher describes is realized. The eschatological feast (Revelation 19:9: the marriage supper of the Lamb) is the fulfillment of eating 'for strength, not excess': abundance without indulgence in the kingdom that never ends.
18: A theme: laziness causes the structural collapse of the house. The image of the leaking roof and sagging rafters is applied in the NT's parable of the talents (Matthew 25:14-30) and the warning against neglecting the great salvation (Hebrews 2:3). Christ himself is the cornerstone who holds the building together (Ephesians 2:20; 1 Peter 2:6); the household of God built on him does not sag, while every house built on any other foundation eventually falls (Matthew 7:24-27).
19: A theme: feast for laughter, wine for gladness, money for everything — the Preacher's realistic assessment of creaturely life's goods. These goods are real; the NT does not negate them but transforms them. Christ's first miracle was providing wine at a feast (John 2:1-11); his last meal became the pattern for the church's worship (Luke 22:19-20); and the eschatological banquet (Revelation 19:9) is the permanent form of the feasting that 'under the sun' is only temporary.
20: A revelation of God: do not curse the king even in your thoughts — a bird may carry your words. All speech is accountable; nothing is hidden from the one who hears. Christ intensifies this principle: 'I tell you that everyone will have to give account on the day of judgment for every empty word they have spoken' (Matthew 12:36). The bird of Ecclesiastes becomes the perfect Judge of the NT: not only outward speech but the inner thoughts that produce it will be brought to light (1 Corinthians 4:5).
